source.title: HLA-B*5701 screening for hypersensitivity to abacavir (PREDICT-1 trial)
source.finding: Prospective HLA-B*5701 screening eliminated immunologically confirmed abacavir hypersensitivity (0% vs 2.7% in controls, P<0.001). 94% of patients do not carry the allele and are at low risk.

GG.effect: Very high risk of severe, potentially life-threatening abacavir hypersensitivity reaction. Symptoms include fever, rash, GI distress, respiratory issues within first 6 weeks.
GG.recommendation: DO NOT prescribe abacavir. Use alternative NRTI (tenofovir, emtricitabine). Record as abacavir allergy in medical record.

GT.effect: High risk of severe abacavir hypersensitivity reaction. One copy of the risk allele is sufficient to trigger the immune-mediated reaction.
GT.recommendation: DO NOT prescribe abacavir. Use alternative NRTI. Record as abacavir allergy.

TT.effect: Low risk of immunologically confirmed abacavir hypersensitivity. Standard abacavir use is appropriate.
TT.recommendation: Abacavir can be prescribed. Maintain standard clinical vigilance for any hypersensitivity symptoms.
